title: Storming of the Bastille
summary: The Bastille's fall became the symbolic breach through which old-regime authority collapsed in public view.
publicSummary: The fall of the Bastille became the moment when the old regime visibly lost its grip.
destroyedOrDisplaced: the aura of untouchable monarchical order
sourcesOrRationale: Britannica coverage of the French Revolution and Bastille-centered revolutionary rupture.

title: Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen
summary: The Declaration articulated universal rights and popular sovereignty as the moral foundation of the new order.
publicSummary: The Declaration set out universal rights and popular rule as the moral basis of the new France.
destroyedOrDisplaced: the principle of birthright privilege as political default
sourcesOrRationale: Britannica: Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen.

title: Reign of Terror
summary: Revolutionary emergency hardened into coercive virtue politics, making fear and suspicion part of state power.
publicSummary: Emergency rule hardened into fear, suspicion, and coercion, turning terror into part of state power.
destroyedOrDisplaced: faith that revolutionary virtue could remain non-coercive
sourcesOrRationale: Britannica coverage of the French Revolution's radical phase and Year II emergency.

title: Coup of 18 Brumaire
summary: Napoleon's coup ended the Directory and opened a more centralized and executive-led settlement.
publicSummary: Napoleon's coup ended the Directory and opened a more centralised order led from the top.
destroyedOrDisplaced: late-revolutionary republican instability
sourcesOrRationale: Britannica coverage of Napoleon and the post-revolutionary settlement.

title: Napoleonic Code
summary: The Civil Code turned parts of the revolutionary legacy into durable legal structure under centralized state authority.
publicSummary: The Civil Code turned part of the Revolution's legacy into lasting law under a stronger central state.
consequences: revolutionary principles narrowed into legal order
destroyedOrDisplaced: older patchwork legal order
sourcesOrRationale: Britannica coverage of the Napoleonic Code.

title: Waterloo and Imperial Collapse
summary: Military defeat at Waterloo ended Napoleon's return and broke the legitimacy of empire-through-glory.
publicSummary: Defeat at Waterloo ended Napoleon's return and broke the idea that empire and glory could hold France together.
destroyedOrDisplaced: Napoleonic claim to stable continental order
sourcesOrRationale: Britannica coverage of Waterloo and the fall of Napoleon.

title: Bourbon Restoration Settlement
summary: The monarchy returned, but it did so in a society that could no longer simply resume pre-revolutionary legitimacy.
publicSummary: The monarchy came back, but France could no longer simply return to the world before the Revolution.
destroyedOrDisplaced: the idea of effortless return to old-regime normality
sourcesOrRationale: Britannica coverage of Restoration France.

title: July Revolution
summary: The July Revolution toppled Charles X and demonstrated again that monarchy remained vulnerable to urban and constitutional rupture.
publicSummary: The July Revolution toppled Charles X and showed again how vulnerable monarchy was to revolt and constitutional crisis.
destroyedOrDisplaced: confidence in Restoration permanence
sourcesOrRationale: Britannica coverage of the July Revolution and 19th-century France.

title: February Revolution and Second Republic
summary: The 1848 revolution brought down the July Monarchy and reopened the republic as a democratic and social possibility.
publicSummary: The 1848 revolution brought down the July Monarchy and reopened the republic as a democratic possibility.
destroyedOrDisplaced: the legitimacy of the July Monarchy
sourcesOrRationale: Britannica coverage of the 1848 Revolution in France.

title: Coup of Louis-Napoleon and the Second Empire
summary: Louis-Napoleon's coup and imperial refounding turned republican instability into a new executive-authoritarian order.
publicSummary: Louis-Napoleon's coup turned republican instability into a new authoritarian order led from the executive.
destroyedOrDisplaced: hope for a stable democratic republic in 1848
sourcesOrRationale: Britannica coverage of Louis-Napoleon and the Second Empire.

title: Franco-Prussian War and Collapse of the Second Empire
summary: Defeat in the Franco-Prussian War shattered imperial legitimacy and forced another regime reset.
publicSummary: Defeat in the Franco-Prussian War shattered imperial rule and forced France into another regime reset.
destroyedOrDisplaced: Bonapartist legitimacy through prestige and strength
sourcesOrRationale: Britannica coverage of the Franco-Prussian War and regime collapse.

title: Constitutional Laws of the Third Republic
summary: The 1875 constitutional laws stabilized the Third Republic as a parliamentary regime durable enough to outlast repeated crises.
publicSummary: The 1875 constitutional laws stabilised the Third Republic and gave France a more durable parliamentary system.
destroyedOrDisplaced: the expectation of monarchical restoration
sourcesOrRationale: Britannica: The constitution of the Third Republic and Third Republic overview.

title: Dreyfus Affair
summary: The Dreyfus Affair exposed anti-Semitism, army prestige, and the fragility of republican justice in a deeply divided society.
publicSummary: The Dreyfus Affair exposed anti-Semitism, military prestige, and how fragile justice could be in a divided republic.
destroyedOrDisplaced: confidence that the republic's institutions were politically neutral
sourcesOrRationale: Britannica: Dreyfus Affair.

title: Law on the Separation of Churches and State
summary: The 1905 law made secular republican authority a more explicit foundation of public life.
publicSummary: The 1905 law made secular republican authority a more explicit foundation of public life.
destroyedOrDisplaced: clerical claim to privileged public authority
sourcesOrRationale: Britannica coverage of the Third Republic and secular nation-building.

title: Popular Front Government
summary: The Popular Front tried to answer crisis with reform, labour concessions, and democratic defense, but did so under mounting strain.
publicSummary: The Popular Front answered crisis with reform, labour gains, and democratic defence, but under growing strain.
destroyedOrDisplaced: the assumption that crisis only strengthened the right
sourcesOrRationale: Britannica coverage of interwar France and the Popular Front.

title: Fall of France and Vichy Regime
summary: Military defeat in 1940 broke the Third Republic and shifted legitimacy toward Vichy, collaboration, and resistance at once.
publicSummary: Defeat in 1940 broke the Third Republic and split France between Vichy rule, collaboration, and resistance.
destroyedOrDisplaced: the authority of prewar republican continuity
sourcesOrRationale: Britannica: France since 1940 and Vichy-era coverage.

title: Liberation of Paris and Provisional Refounding
summary: The liberation of Paris and de Gaulle's provisional authority reattached republican legitimacy to resistance and reconstruction.
publicSummary: The liberation of Paris and de Gaulle's provisional rule tied the republic again to resistance and rebuilding.
destroyedOrDisplaced: Vichy claims to national legitimacy
sourcesOrRationale: Britannica: France since 1940, Liberation section.

title: May 1958 Crisis and Collapse of the Fourth Republic
summary: The Algerian crisis triggered the collapse of the Fourth Republic and cleared the way for de Gaulle's return and constitutional redesign.
publicSummary: The Algerian crisis brought down the Fourth Republic and cleared the way for de Gaulle's return and a new constitution.
destroyedOrDisplaced: the claim that parliamentary instability could manage empire crisis
sourcesOrRationale: Britannica coverage of the Fourth Republic and Algeria-driven crisis.

title: Constitution of the Fifth Republic
summary: The new constitution increased executive power and created a more durable institutional framework for postwar France.
publicSummary: The new constitution strengthened executive power and created a more durable postwar system.
destroyedOrDisplaced: the central weakness of the Fourth Republic model
sourcesOrRationale: Britannica: Fifth Republic and France since 1940.

title: Algerian Independence
summary: The end of the Algerian War closed a core imperial struggle while forcing France to recalibrate identity, authority, and post-imperial politics.
publicSummary: The end of the Algerian War closed a central imperial struggle and forced France to rethink identity, power, and post-imperial politics.
consequences: Fifth Republic stabilized after empire crisis
destroyedOrDisplaced: the idea that Algeria could remain the unquestioned extension of the French polity
sourcesOrRationale: Britannica: France since 1940 and Algerian War coverage.

title: Events of May 1968
summary: Student revolt and mass strike challenged technocratic order, hierarchy, and cultural authority at national scale.
publicSummary: Student revolt and mass strike challenged hierarchy, authority, and the technocratic order across France.
destroyedOrDisplaced: confidence that modernization alone secured obedience
sourcesOrRationale: Britannica: events of May 1968.

title: Election of François Mitterrand
summary: Mitterrand's election opened a left-led reform moment inside the Fifth Republic and reset public expectations about the state's social role.
publicSummary: Mitterrand's election opened a left-led reform moment and changed what people expected the state to do socially.
consequences: later adjustment to European and market constraints
destroyedOrDisplaced: the assumption that the Fifth Republic would remain structurally center-right
sourcesOrRationale: Britannica: François Mitterrand and France under a Socialist presidency.

title: Maastricht Referendum and European Integration Strain
summary: France's narrow approval of Maastricht showed that Europe would become a recurring sovereignty and legitimacy divide inside the republic.
publicSummary: France's narrow approval of Maastricht showed that Europe would become a recurring divide over sovereignty and trust.
destroyedOrDisplaced: the idea that European integration was politically unifying by default
sourcesOrRationale: Britannica: Maastricht Treaty and France since 1940.

title: Paris Attacks of 2015 and State-of-Emergency Politics
summary: The 2015 attacks intensified security politics, identity strain, and the republic's debate over liberty, protection, and belonging.
publicSummary: The 2015 attacks intensified security politics, identity strain, and arguments over liberty, protection, and belonging.
madePossible: deeper legitimacy contest over protection and pluralism
destroyedOrDisplaced: the assumption that late-modern French security politics could remain peripheral
sourcesOrRationale: Britannica: Paris attacks of 2015 and France since 1940.
